skill.name: sharing-skills
skill.description: Guides contributors through preparing, committing, pushing, and opening pull requests for reusable AI skills.
skill.summary: Share reusable AI skills through a structured Git and GitHub pull request workflow.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell command patterns, home directory paths, and two documentation URLs in SKILL.md. Review confirms these are contribution workflow examples, not hidden execution or exfiltration, but they can change local and remote Git state if followed incorrectly.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Changing Shell Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill provides Git and GitHub CLI commands for checkout, pull, push, branch deletion, force push, and pull request creation. This is legitimate for a contribution guide, but users must review remotes and branch names before running the commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples, so there is no hidden execution. The risk is still real because running them can modify local branches and remote repositories.

medium_findings.title: Hidden Home Directory Workflow Path
medium_findings.description: The instructions use ~/.config/superpowers/skills as the working directory. This is expected for the documented tool, but marketplace users should understand that the workflow operates in a hidden directory under the user's home folder.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The home directory access is explicit and tied to the skill repository location. It is not secret collection, but it is a filesystem risk factor.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The skill links to GitHub CLI and GitHub SSH documentation. These are ordinary support links and do not show data exfiltration or unauthorized network behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation references in troubleshooting text. No code sends data to these URLs.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Matches
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the cited lines contain description and testing prose. No evidence found of cryptographic code or weak algorithm use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are plain Markdown text and shell heredoc content. There is no cryptographic API, hash function, cipher, or secret handling.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Git And GitHub Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes bash examples that invoke git and gh. The pattern is appropriate for the workflow, but it can publish commits, create pull requests, delete remote branches, or force-push changes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present in Markdown code blocks. Their purpose is legitimate, but several commands change remote repository state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution In Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples use command substitution to build commit and pull request bodies. The content is fixed documentation text, but users should avoid inserting untrusted shell content into these patterns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The substitution is visible and limited to heredoc examples. Injection risk depends on user modification, so confidence is moderate.

content.value_statement: Contributors need a repeatable way to send reusable skills upstream without mixing unrelated changes. This skill provides a clear Git and GitHub workflow for branch, commit, push, and pull request steps.
actual_capabilities: Explains when a skill is broad enough to share upstream.
Provides a branch-based Git workflow for skill contributions.
Shows commit message and pull request body structure.
Covers GitHub CLI pull request creation.
Includes post-merge cleanup and branch deletion steps.
Lists troubleshooting steps for GitHub CLI and SSH access.
limitations: It does not validate skill quality or run tests automatically.
It assumes GitHub CLI is installed and authenticated.
It uses placeholder upstream repository names that users must replace.
It does not protect users from incorrect branch or remote choices.

use_cases.title: Submit a New Reusable Skill
use_cases.description: Prepare one finished skill for upstream review with a focused branch and pull request.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Contributions
use_cases.description: Give team members a shared process for proposing skills without batching unrelated work.

use_cases.title: Clean Up After Merge
use_cases.description: Sync local main and remove feature branches after an accepted contribution.

prompt_templates.title: Check Sharing Readiness
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review before creating a branch.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my skill idea and tell me if it should be shared upstream or kept personal.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Contribution Steps
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through preparing a single skill contribution using the sharing-skills workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Pull Request Text
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a clear pull request title, summary, testing section, and context section for this skill.

prompt_templates.title: Review Merge Cleanup
prompt_templates.prompt: Walk me through post-merge cleanup and highlight commands that can change remote branches.

output_examples.input: I built a reusable skill and want to share it.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks whether the skill is general, tested, documented, and free of sensitive information before recommending a contribution path.

output_examples.input: I need a pull request description for my skill.
output_examples.output: The assistant produces a concise summary, testing notes, and context section that explain the skill's purpose and review scope.

output_examples.input: My pull request was merged. What next?
output_examples.output: The assistant explains local main synchronization, feature branch cleanup, and remote branch deletion with review warnings.

best_practices: Share one skill per pull request.
Review repository remotes before pushing changes.
Document how the skill was tested before opening the pull request.
anti_patterns: Do not batch multiple unrelated skills in one pull request.
Do not share skills that include secrets or organization-specific details.
Do not run force-push or branch deletion commands without checking the target.

faq.question: Does this skill create pull requests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides the workflow and command examples for a user-controlled GitHub CLI process.

faq.question: Can I use it without GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: The documented workflow assumes GitHub CLI. You can adapt the review structure to the GitHub web interface.

faq.question: Should I share project-specific skills?
faq.answer: No. The skill recommends sharing only broadly useful skills without sensitive or organization-specific content.

faq.question: Does it test my skill?
faq.answer: No. It expects the skill to be tested before the sharing workflow begins.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes visible Git commands that can push, delete, or force-push branches if used incorrectly.

faq.question: Is there evidence of malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The commands support the documented contribution workflow.
